abstract:
Village Institutes represent a cornerstone of Turkish Republican educational history, originally established in 1940 to train teachers for rural development. As the era’s health demands grew and national policies evolved, an urgent need for a specialized health workforce emerged. In response, a "Health Branch" was integrated into the Village Institute model in 1943, designed to train village health officers who would serve alongside teachers in neglected rural areas. 

This study reconstructs the professional lives and educational backgrounds of these officers through primary source materials and oral histories. We conducted interviews with four retired village health officers—the last of whom graduated in 1951—and preserved their testimonies through detailed field notes. Furthermore, we analyzed a unique collection of personal documents provided by these individuals, including student identity cards, period-specific medical textbooks, official diplomas, Sahra Medical Service School certifications, and specialized anesthesia training permits. 

By triangulating these findings with existing literature and legislative archives, the research demonstrates that village health officers received a rigorous, high-quality education tailored specifically to the complex medical needs of rural Anatolia. Beyond clinical duties, they played a pivotal role in public health advocacy and epidemic prevention. By bringing these often-overlooked health professionals to the forefront, this paper clarifies nuanced historical details and serves as a vital lens for understanding the educational philosophy, health policies, and social fabric of a transformative period in Turkish history.
